# Quickstart: Telegram News Digest Assistant

**Feature**: 001-telegram-news-assistant
**Date**: 2026-06-21
**Purpose**: a runnable end-to-end validation that proves the feature works. This guide is a *validation* document, not a build document — the implementation itself lives in the tasks generated by `/speckit.tasks` and the source tree under `backend/` and `frontend/`.

## What you will validate

By the end of this quickstart, you will have:

1. A working Go binary that runs the cycle and serves the admin API.
2. A built Svelte admin panel served from the same binary.
3. A real Telegram bot talking to a real chat (or a local Telegram fake) and delivering a real digest message.
4. An end-to-end check that all 11 success criteria (SC-001…SC-011) are observable.

The guide covers three validation tracks, in order of how quickly you can run them:

- **Track A — Pure-local (no Telegram, no AI)**: 5 minutes. Validates the cycle, the store, the renderer, the admin API, and the SPA. Uses the in-process `fake` AI summarizer and a `fake` Telegram client.
- **Track B — Real Telegram + fake AI**: 20 minutes. Validates the Telegram send path and the read path against `getChat`. Uses `@BotFather` and a public test channel.
- **Track C — Real Telegram + real AI**: 30 minutes. Validates end-to-end with a real OpenAI-compatible provider.

## Prerequisites

- **Go 1.22+** (`go version`).
- **Node 20+** and **npm** (`node --version && npm --version`).
- **make** (any GNU make).
- **curl** and **jq** for hitting the admin API.
- A POSIX shell (Git Bash on Windows, or WSL). The Makefile uses `cp` and `mkdir -p`; if you are on native Windows PowerShell, use the `make` from Git for Windows or run the commands manually.
- A Telegram account (Track B and C only) and a channel the bot can read.
- An OpenAI-compatible API key (Track C only).

## 0. Clone and build (all tracks)

```bash
# from the repo root
make deps        # go mod download + npm ci
make build       # builds the Svelte SPA, then `go build` the embedded binary
```

The resulting binary is at `bin/assistant`. Confirm:

```bash
./bin/assistant --version     # prints the version
./bin/assistant --help        # prints the available flags
```

## Track A — Pure-local validation (≈ 5 min)

This track runs the service with no network dependencies. It uses the `fake` AI summarizer and an in-process `fake` Telegram client that records sent messages to a JSONL file on disk.

### A1. Start the service

```bash
mkdir -p .runtime
cat > .runtime/assistant.env <<'EOF'
ASSISTANT_AI_PROVIDER=fake
DIGEST_INTERVAL=10s
ADMIN_LISTEN_ADDR=127.0.0.1:8080
DB_PATH=./.runtime/assistant.db
TELEGRAM_FAKE_OUT=./.runtime/telegram-sent.jsonl
EOF

set -a; source .runtime/assistant.env; set +a
./bin/assistant &
SERVICE_PID=$!
sleep 2
```

### A2. Open the admin panel

Navigate to <http://127.0.0.1:8080/>. You should see:

- The **Overview** page showing `Last successful cycle: never`, `Scheduler: idle`, `DB: ok`.
- A **Channels** page with an empty list and an "Add channel" form.
- A **Categories** page listing the six default categories (Politics, Technology, Business, Sports, World, Other).
- A **Settings** page showing the interval (`10s`).

### A3. Add a channel

```bash
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/channels \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"handle":"sample_news"}' | jq .
```

The `fake` Telegram client accepts any well-formed handle. The response should be `201 Created` with the new channel. The admin panel should now show one channel with `status: active`.

### A4. Seed a synthetic post (fake client only)

The fake client reads source messages from a YAML file at `./.runtime/source-messages.yaml`:

```bash
cat > .runtime/source-messages.yaml <<'EOF'
- channel: sample_news
  messages:
    - id: 1001
      text: "Telegram rolls out scheduled messages in channels."
      media: text
    - id: 1002
      text: "EU parliament passes the AI Liability Directive."
      media: text
    - id: 1003
      text: "A new open-source LLM beats GPT-4 on a public benchmark."
      media: text
EOF
```

Restart the service so the fake client reloads the seed file:

```bash
kill $SERVICE_PID; wait $SERVICE_PID 2>/dev/null
set -a; source .runtime/assistant.env; set +a
./bin/assistant &
SERVICE_PID=$!
```

### A5. Wait for one cycle

Because the interval is `10s`, within ~12 seconds the service runs a cycle. Verify:

```bash
# 1. The sent file has one entry
test "$(wc -l < .runtime/telegram-sent.jsonl)" -ge 1 && echo OK

# 2. The cycle is recorded
curl -sS 'http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/cycles?limit=1' | jq '.cycles[0] | {status, input_msg_count, output_items, degraded}'

# Expected: { "status": "succeeded", "input_msg_count": 3, "output_items": 3, "degraded": false }
```

### A6. Read the digest from the admin history view

Navigate to <http://127.0.0.1:8080/history>. Open the most recent cycle. The rendered text should match the format in `contracts/telegram-render.md`:

```
📰 News digest — <window_end UTC>

# Politics
• EU parliament passes the AI Liability Directive.  _(sample_news)_

# Technology
• Telegram rolls out scheduled messages in channels.  _(sample_news)_
• A new open-source LLM beats GPT-4 on a public benchmark.  _(sample_news)_

— cycle <short_id> · 3 items · ok
```

(Exact category assignment is decided by the fake AI in `internal/ai/fake.go`; the contract is that the digest is grouped, not that the specific category per item is fixed.)

### A7. Validate the success criteria you can validate here

| SC | How to validate in Track A | Expected |
|---|---|---|
| **SC-002** (no message when no items) | Stop the seed file, wait 2 cycles, check `.runtime/telegram-sent.jsonl` does not grow | unchanged |
| **SC-005** (channel change reflected next cycle) | Add a second channel via the admin panel, wait one cycle, see its messages | new channel appears in the next digest |
| **SC-006** (category change reflected next cycle) | Rename the "Politics" category to "Policy", wait one cycle | new digest uses "Policy" as the heading |
| **SC-008** (no double-deliver on restart) | `kill $SERVICE_PID; ./bin/assistant &` mid-window, wait two cycles | exactly one digest per window in `digests` |
| **SC-010** (config survives restart) | `kill $SERVICE_PID; ./bin/assistant &`, check channel list and settings | unchanged |
| **SC-011** (single Telegram message for ≤ 50 items) | Seed 30 messages, wait one cycle, inspect `digests.rendered_text` | one row, `degraded = 0` |

SC-001, SC-003, SC-004, SC-007, SC-009 require a real Telegram send and / or a real timing measurement and are validated in Tracks B and C.

### A8. Tear down

```bash
kill $SERVICE_PID; wait $SERVICE_PID 2>/dev/null
rm -rf .runtime
```

---

## Track B — Real Telegram + fake AI (≈ 20 min)

This track uses a real `@BotFather` token and a real public channel the bot has joined. The AI summarizer stays fake so the validation does not depend on a paid API.

### B1. Create a bot and a test channel

1. In Telegram, talk to `@BotFather`, send `/newbot`, follow the prompts. Save the token.
2. Create a public channel (e.g. `@my_quickstart_channel`). Make the bot an administrator with at least "Post messages" permission (or just add it as a member; the read API works for members of public channels).
3. Post 2–3 messages in the channel manually.

### B2. Get your chat id

Send any message to your bot, then:

```bash
curl -sS "https://api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/getUpdates" | jq '.result[-1].message.chat.id'
```

Use that integer as `TELEGRAM_SUBSCRIBER_CHAT` below.

### B3. Start the service with real Telegram, fake AI

```bash
cat > .runtime/assistant.env <<EOF
ASSISTANT_AI_PROVIDER=fake
DIGEST_INTERVAL=1m
ADMIN_LISTEN_ADDR=127.0.0.1:8080
DB_PATH=./.runtime/assistant.db
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}
TELEGRAM_SUBSCRIBER_CHAT=${TELEGRAM_SUBSCRIBER_CHAT}
EOF
chmod 600 .runtime/assistant.env

set -a; source .runtime/assistant.env; set +a
./bin/assistant &
SERVICE_PID=$!
```

### B4. Add the channel via the API

```bash
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/channels \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"handle":"my_quickstart_channel"}' | jq .
```

The server calls `getChat` to confirm existence. A `201` confirms the bot can see the channel. A `bot_not_in_channel` error means you forgot to add the bot as a member.

### B5. Post a message in the channel and wait

Post one new message in `@my_quickstart_channel`, then wait up to one interval + ~10s. Verify you receive a Telegram message from the bot in your personal chat.

### B6. Validate

- **SC-001** (digest within interval + margin): measure `telegram-sent-at - source-posted-at` from the API:
  ```bash
  curl -sS "http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/cycles/$(curl -sS 'http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/cycles?limit=1' | jq -r '.cycles[0].id')" | \
    jq '{window_start, window_end, finished_at, sent_at: .digest.sent_at}'
  ```
  Expect `sent_at - max(source_posted_at) ≤ interval + 60s`.
- **SC-009** (admin panel latency): `time curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/channels > /dev/null` — expect < 500 ms p95 over 10 runs.
- **SC-015** (failures surfaced): block the bot from your personal chat, wait one cycle, check `GET /api/events?limit=5` — expect a `telegram.send.blocked` event.

### B7. Tear down

```bash
kill $SERVICE_PID; wait $SERVICE_PID 2>/dev/null
rm -rf .runtime
# revoke the bot token via @BotFather /revoke
```

---

## Track C — Real Telegram + real AI (≈ 30 min)

Same as Track B, with the AI summarizer pointed at a real OpenAI-compatible provider.

```bash
cat > .runtime/assistant.env <<EOF
ASSISTANT_AI_PROVIDER=openai
DIGEST_INTERVAL=10m
ADMIN_LISTEN_ADDR=127.0.0.1:8080
DB_PATH=./.runtime/assistant.db
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}
TELEGRAM_SUBSCRIBER_CHAT=${TELEGRAM_SUBSCRIBER_CHAT}
AI_BASE_URL=${AI_BASE_URL}
AI_MODEL=${AI_MODEL}
AI_API_KEY=${AI_API_KEY}
EOF
chmod 600 .runtime/assistant.env

set -a; source .runtime/assistant.env; set +a
./bin/assistant &
SERVICE_PID=$!
```

Add the channel, post a long message in it, wait one interval, verify the digest you receive is a true summary (not a verbatim copy — SC-003) by comparing character counts: the summary should be ≤ 280 chars even when the source is 2 KB+.

For SC-007 (degraded mode), simulate an AI outage by pointing `AI_BASE_URL` to an unreachable host and re-launching. Within one cycle you should still receive a digest, marked `degraded (AI unavailable)` in the footer and in `digests.degraded = 1` in the API.

### Tear down

```bash
kill $SERVICE_PID; wait $SERVICE_PID 2>/dev/null
rm -rf .runtime
```

---

## Mapping back to the spec

| Spec item | Validated by |
|---|---|
| FR-001…FR-006 (channels, fetch, interval, AI, category) | Track A steps A3–A6, Track B step B5 |
| FR-007, FR-008 (deliver vs suppress) | Track A step A7 (SC-002, SC-011) |
| FR-009 (dedup) | Track A with two channels sharing the same forwarded message in the seed YAML |
| FR-010 (size limit) | Track A with 60+ seeded messages, inspect `digests.rendered_text` length |
| FR-011, FR-012 (admin panel + operator settings) | All tracks, step "Open the admin panel" |
| FR-013 (persistence) | SC-010 in Track A step A7 |
| FR-014 (history) | All tracks, `GET /api/cycles` and the History page |
| FR-015 (failure surfacing) | Track B step B6 (SC-015-style) and Track C SC-007 |
| FR-016 (restart safety) | SC-008 in Track A step A7 |
| FR-017 (non-text) | Track A seed YAML with `media: image` and a caption |
| FR-018 (pluggable AI) | Switching `ASSISTANT_AI_PROVIDER` between `fake` and `openai` without code changes |
| All success criteria SC-001…SC-011 | covered above per track |

## What this quickstart does NOT do

- It does not implement the service. Use `/speckit.tasks` and the source tree (`backend/`, `frontend/`) for that.
- It does not load-test the cycle. SC-004 (95% cycle success) needs an external load driver; this guide proves the cycle path works once, not that it survives a 7-day soak.
- It does not exercise the on-the-wire contracts under failure injection. The contract specs in `contracts/` are validated by the per-component test suites (renderer golden files, admin API request/response tests, AI interface contract tests), not by this guide.
